title: Kittens from birth to weaning
subtitle: Managing a meat rabbit doe through kindling and the first eight weeks.
excerpt: A doe giving birth needs minimal intervention but careful observation. This covers the nest preparation, the kindling check, the first-week mortality risk, and the 28-day weaning process for a colony of meat rabbits.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Rabbits Kept for Meat (2019). Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), rabbit-rearing chapter.

content.text: placed five to seven days before her due date (gestation is 31 to 32 days from mating). The nest box should fit snugly into one end of her cage with a low front threshold she can step over easily. Line it with clean dry hay; the doe will pull belly fur to insulate the nest in the final day before
Check the nest box within a few hours of kindling. Remove any stillborn

and any membranes the doe has not consumed. Count the live kits and note whether they are warm (a good sign the doe has covered them) or cold and scattered (a sign the doe has not accepted the nest). A doe that scatters her kits outside the box on the first day usually settles into nest-building behaviour within 24 hours if the environment is quiet and free from disturbance.

Rabbit does do not sit in the nest box; they visit once or twice daily to nurse. This is normal behaviour from a prey species that does not draw predators to the nest by lingering. Check the kits daily at the same time: warm kits with rounded bellies are feeding; cold, flat-bellied kits are not. A kit that has not fed by the end of day two is at risk.

Eyes open at 10 to 12 days. Kits start exploring the cage from week two and begin nibbling the doe's pellets and hay from day 18 to 21. Ensure the food and water are accessible at a low enough height for the kits. Providing a second water source at kit height from day 18 onward prevents competition for the doe's drinker.

Wean at 28 to 32 days by moving the doe out of the cage rather than the kits. The kits stay in the familiar environment; the doe moves to a new cage. Separating the doe removes the risk of the kits being pressed into early re-mating, and the kits remain less stressed in a space they already know. Remove the nest box at weaning. Ensure feed and water for the kits are topped up and available from the first hours without the doe.
